request.question: Who is a retired American track and field athlete, who mostly competed in the shot put who returned four years later to the most populous metropolitan area in the world ?

title: Maren Seidler
Jillian Camarena-Williams
Gerhard Windolf
Tokyo
Marianne Werner
Raven Saunders
Dallas Long
Neal Steinhauer
Charlie Fonville
Randy Matson
sentences: Maren Elizabeth Seidler (born June 11, 1951 in Brooklyn, New York) is a retired American track and field athlete.
She dominated the shot put from the mid 1960s through 1980.
She won the event at the USA Outdoor Track and Field Championships eleven times starting in 1967, including nine in a row from 1972 to 1980.
She was the American champion indoors nine times, 1968-9, 1972, 1974-5 and 1977 to 1980.
She won her event at the United States Olympic Trials four straight times 1968-1980, a feat only equalled by only one woman, Madeline Manning, Edwin Moses is the only man to achieve four.
Jackie Joyner Kersee is the only woman who has won more events at the Olympic Trials, split between the long jump and heptathlon.
She competed in the Olympics three times, making the final twice.
Her 1980 selection was quashed by the 1980 Summer Olympics boycott.
Seidler did however receive one of 461 Congressional Gold Medals created especially for the spurned athletes.
Jillian Mary "Jill" Camarena-Williams (born 2 March 1982) is a retired American track and field athlete who competes in the shot put.
She competed at the 2012 and 2008 Beijing Olympics and has represented the United States both indoor and outdoors at World Championship-level.
Gerhard Windolf (born January 13, 1924) is a German Masters track and field athlete.
He was the 1995 Masters World Champion in the decathlon, beating future world record holder Pekka Penttila.
Four years later he returned to get a bronze medal behind Penttila.
As a combined event athlete, he also has produced medal winning results in a range of other events, primarily jumping events.
He won the world championship in the high jump in 1995, 2005, 2009, and 2015, while getting bronze in 1991 and 1999.
He won the long jump in 2009 and 2015, with a bronze in 1999.
He also picked up a silver in the triple jump and shot put in 2009, with bronze medals in 2005 and 2015.
Tokyo (] , ), officially Tokyo Metropolis, is the capital of Japan and one of its 47 prefectures.
The Greater Tokyo Area is the most populous metropolitan area in the world.
It is the seat of the Emperor of Japan and the Japanese government.
Tokyo is in the Kantō region on the southeastern side of the main island Honshu and includes the Izu Islands and Ogasawara Islands.
Formerly known as Edo, it has been the de facto seat of government since 1603 when Shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu made the city his headquarters.
It officially became the capital after Emperor Meiji moved his seat to the city from the old capital of Kyoto in 1868; at that time Edo was renamed Tokyo.
Tokyo Metropolis was formed in 1943 from the merger of the former and the .
Marianne Werner, née Schulze Entrup, later Ader (born 4 January 1924), is a retired West German athlete who competed mainly in the shot put events.
She was born in Dülmen.
Werner competed for West Germany at the 1952 Summer Olympics, held in Helsinki, Finland, in the Women's Shot Put, where she won the silver medal.

Four years later she returned with the United Team of Germany to Melbourne for the 1956 Summer Olympics where she won the bronze medal one place behind Soviet Galina Zybina for the second Olympics and eventual winner, another Soviet Tamara Tyshkevich.
Raven Saunders (born May 15, 1996) is an American track and field athlete who competes in the shot put and discus throw.
She won three NCAA collegiate titles in the shot put for the University of Mississippi.
She was a world junior medalist in 2014 and the Pan American junior champion in 2015.
She holds a personal record of 19.33 m (63 ft floor(((19.33)*3.28084)−(floor((19.33)*3.28084)) *12) in) for the shot put.
Dallas Crutcher Long (born June 13, 1940) is a retired American track and field athlete, who mostly competed in the shot put.
Between 1959 and 1964 he set six official and five unofficial world records.
Long attended the University of Southern California.
He competed at the 1960 Summer Olympics, where he won a bronze medal behind fellow Americans Bill Nieder and Parry O'Brien.
He returned four years later to Tokyo for the 1964 Summer Olympics and won a gold medal.
Domestically he won the AAU title in 1961 and the NCAA title in 1960–62.
Neal Steinhauer (born August 18, 1944) is a retired American track and field athlete.

He won a silver medal in the shot put at the 1967 Pan American Games, behind Randy Matson, and also won two medals at the 1967 Summer Universiade in Tokyo (gold in the shot put and bronze in the discus).
A one-time American national champion (in 1969).
Steinhauer set his personal best (21.01 m) on March 25, 1967 at a meet in Sacramento.
He was on the cover of the February 1969 issue of "Track and Field News."
Charles Edward "Charlie" Fonville (April 27, 1927 – July 13, 1994) was an American track and field athlete who set a world record in the shot put.
In 1945, he had been named the Michigan High School Track & Field Athlete of the Year.
He won the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) shot put championship in 1947 and 1948.
Competing for the University of Michigan at the Kansas Relays in April 1948, Fonville broke a 14-year-old world record, throwing the shot a foot further than the record.
James Randel "Randy" Matson (born March 5, 1945) is an American track and field athlete who mostly competed in the shot put.
He won a silver medal at the 1964 and a gold medal at the 1968 Olympics.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

He returned four years later to Tokyo for the 1964 Summer Olympics and won a gold medal.  Domestically he won the AAU title in 1961 and the NCAA title in 1960–62.

Competing for the University of Michigan at the Kansas Relays in April 1948, Fonville broke a 14-year-old world record, throwing the shot a foot further than the record.

Randy Matson | James Randel "Randy" Matson (born March 5, 1945) is an American track and field athlete who mostly competed in the shot put.  He won a silver medal at the 1964 and a gold medal at the 1968 Olympics.

Jillian Camarena-Williams | Jillian Mary "Jill" Camarena-Williams (born 2 March 1982) is a retired American track and field athlete who competes in the shot put.  She competed at the 2012 and 2008 Beijing Olympics and has represented the United States both indoor and outdoors at World Championship-level.

Werner competed for West Germany at the 1952 Summer Olympics, held in Helsinki, Finland, in the Women's Shot Put, where she won the silver medal.  Four years later she returned with the United Team of Germany to Melbourne for the 1956 Summer Olympics where she won the bronze medal one place behind Soviet Galina Zybina for the second Olympics and eventual winner, another Soviet Tamara Tyshkevich.

response.summary_1: Passage [1] states: "Dallas Crutcher Long (born June 13, 1940) is a retired American track and field athlete, who mostly competed in the shot put" and that "He returned four years later to Tokyo for the 1964 Summer Olympics and won a gold medal." Bridge entity: Dallas Long; bridge fact present: he "returned four years later to Tokyo." Missing/next-hop: confirm that "Tokyo" is the "most populous metropolitan area in the world." Retrieval anchors: search or retrieve sources for "Tokyo metropolitan area most populous metropolitan area in the world" or pages stating "Tokyo is the most populous metropolitan area in the world" (e.g., Tokyo metropolitan area population/ranking).
response.hop2_query: Tokyo most populous metropolitan area in the world
hop2_docs: Greater Tokyo Area | The Greater Tokyo Area is the most populous metropolitan area in the world, consisting of the Kantō region of Japan, including the Tokyo Metropolis, as well as the prefecture of Yamanashi.  In Japanese, it is referred to by various terms, one of the most common being National Capital Region (首都圏 , Shuto-ken ) .

List of metropolitan areas in Japan | This is a list of metropolitan areas (都市圏 , toshiken ) in Japan by population as defined by the Statistics Bureau of Japan (SBJ) and the Center for Spatial Information Service of the University of Tokyo.  The region containing most of the people in Japan between Tokyo and Fukuoka is often called the Taiheiyō Belt.

List of metropolitan areas in Asia | This is a list of metropolitan areas and urban areas in Asia with the largest population according to several sources.  The Asian continent is one of the fastest-growing continents in the World, with increasing urbanisation and a high growth rate for cities.  Tokyo in Japan is the world's largest metropolitan area by population.  Many other cities, such as Karachi, Kolkata, Chennai, Dhaka, Delhi, Mumbai, Tehran, Seoul, Shanghai, Manila, Osaka, Beijing, Taipei, Hong Kong, Shenzen, Guangzhou, Bangkok and Jakarta are important metropolises.

Tokyo | Tokyo (] , ), officially Tokyo Metropolis, is the capital of Japan and one of its 47 prefectures.  The Greater Tokyo Area is the most populous metropolitan area in the world.  It is the seat of the Emperor of Japan and the Japanese government.  Tokyo is in the Kantō region on the southeastern side of the main island Honshu and includes the Izu Islands and Ogasawara Islands.  Formerly known as Edo, it has been the de facto seat of government since 1603 when Shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu made the city his headquarters.  It officially became the capital after Emperor Meiji moved his seat to the city from the old capital of Kyoto in 1868; at that time Edo was renamed Tokyo.  Tokyo Metropolis was formed in 1943 from the merger of the former and the .

Keihanshin | Keihanshin (京阪神 , "Kyoto-Osaka-Kobe") is a metropolitan region in Japan encompassing the metropolitan areas of the cities of Kyoto in Kyoto Prefecture, Osaka in Osaka Prefecture and Kobe in Hyōgo Prefecture.  The entire region has a population (as of 2010 ) of 19,341,976 over an area of 13,033 km2 .  It is the second-most-populated urban region in Japan (after the Greater Tokyo area), containing approximately 15% of Japan's population.

Osaka | Osaka (大阪市 , Ōsaka-shi ) (] ;    ) is a designated city in the Kansai region of Japan.  It is the capital city of Osaka Prefecture and the largest component of the Keihanshin Metropolitan Area, the second largest metropolitan area in Japan and among the largest in the world with over 19 million inhabitants.  Situated at the mouth of the Yodo River on Osaka Bay, Osaka is the second largest city in Japan by daytime population after Tokyo's 23 wards and the third largest city by nighttime population after Tokyo's 23 wards and Yokohama, serving as a major economic hub for the country.

Shinjuku | Shinjuku (新宿区 , Shinjuku-ku , "New Lodge") is a special ward in Tokyo, Japan.  It is a major commercial and administrative centre, housing the busiest railway station in the world (Shinjuku Station) and the Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building, the administration centre for the government of Tokyo.  As of 2015, the ward has an estimated population of 337,556 and a population density of 18,517 people per km².  The total area is 18.23 km².

The Greater Tokyo Area (Tokyo) is the most populous metropolitan area in the world.
